name: Najad 460

facts.value: Moderate stiffness; bulb-keel design concentrates ballast for increased stiffness vs. published B/D suggests

sources.title: Najad 460 Center Cockpit Stability Analysis

praise: Solid construction and comfortable stable motion highly praised by owners
Meticulous craftsmanship and attention to detail in joinery and overall finish
Excellent sail plan handling; soft and supple helm; good tracking and decent turn of speed
Spacious and comfortable cockpit with easy access to all running rigging from helm position
Capable bluewater cruising yacht; singlehand-friendly design
Generous headroom (6.46 ft / 1.97 m); spacious and inviting interior
Beautiful hand-laid, sandwich-construction hull with integrated monocoque structure
Engine compartment a work of art with excellent accessibility for maintenance
Well-regarded as capable, seaworthy design suitable for long-distance ocean passages
Rugged hull/deck joint; glued, bolted, and glassed for exceptional strength
complaints: Galley slightly undersized for a boat of this length
Alloy windshield prone to corrosion (though stainless option available)
Some electrical system upgrades needed on older examples (lead-acid to lithium feasible)
known_issues: Alloy windshield corrosion if not maintained; stainless steel optional upgrade recommended
No documented endemic engine/transmission failures on Yanmar 4JH4 powerplant
Minor ergonomic gaps (galley size, head handholds) typical of 2000–2014 era cruisers

provisioning_specific.item: Seldén 224/150 mm Profile Mast Fittings (Tangs, Spreaders, Boom Gooseneck)
provisioning_specific.why: Specific to Najad 460 mast step; stainless steel parts prone to crevice corrosion in saltwater; sourcing through Seldén network or Najad dealers

provisioning_specific.item: Furlex Furling Drum Cartridges (Main + Jib Modules)
provisioning_specific.why: Critical wear items; replacement every 8–10 years typical; stainless foil/rod available; stock spares if extended cruising planned

provisioning_specific.item: Yanmar 4JH4-HTE Fuel Injection Nozzles and Pump Seal Kits
provisioning_specific.why: High-wear items on older engines (post-2000); spares recommended for boats over 5000 running hours; sourcing via Yanmar authorized dealers

provisioning_specific.item: Hull Blister Repair Epoxy and Barrier Coat System
provisioning_specific.why: Najad 460 hull uses sandwich construction with gelcoat; blistering risk increases post-15 years; carry epoxy primer and final coats for preventive repairs

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless Steel Fasteners (M6–M10 Marine Grade A4-70)
provisioning_specific.why: Alloy/stainless mixed-metal design (windshield, deck fittings, rails); galvanic corrosion prevention; carry SS washers, nuts, bolts for deck hardware

provisioning_specific.item: Leak Sealant (Sikaflex 291, Polysulfide) and Bedding Compound Tubes
provisioning_specific.why: Glued/bolted hull/deck joint design; preventive resealing every 5–10 years; carry multiple tubes for remote cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Vane Bearing and Rudder Seal Replacement Kit
provisioning_specific.why: Not explicitly documented as known issue, but center-cockpit steerage and rudder exposure demand spare seals; critical for long cruises

provisioning_specific.item: Water Pump Impeller, Thermostat, and Zinc Anodes (Engine Cooling)
provisioning_specific.why: Yanmar 4JH4 seawater cooling system; impeller wear accelerates in high-salinity cruising; carry 1–2 spares and zinc packs

provisioning_specific.item: Espar Heater Fuel Nozzle, Air Motor, and Glow Plug Cartridges
provisioning_specific.why: Cold-climate cruising essential; spare nozzle and glow plug prevent heating failure in high-latitude passages

provisioning_specific.item: Water Tank and Waste Tank Shut-Off Ball Valves (1.5-inch bronze)
provisioning_specific.why: Dual-tank system (565 L water, 460 L fuel) with through-hull seacocks; carry 2–3 spare ball valves for remote cruising

removed.reason: No credible source. sailboatdata.com returned HTTP 403 — the page could not be verified. The AVS range of 130-150° is explicitly labeled as 'inferred' with no measurement or documentation. The attributed quote ('Ballast to Displacement ratio of about 34.8% indicates reasonable stability') reads as paraphrase, not a verbatim sailboatdata excerpt. Removed as unverifiable inference.
Primary source sailboat.guide returned ECONNREFUSED — the site is unreachable. The quote about bulb keel concentrating ballast for stiffness vs B/D ratio cannot be confirmed. Unverifiable.

sailboat-cruising.com does not mention CE category or Category B certification. The quote attributed to that page ('Powerful, extremely seaworthy hull of modern design that can handle any weather conditions') is not confirmed in the fetched content. No documentary source for CE certification exists in the record.

sailboatdata.com returned 403 — cannot verify ballast weight or keel description from that source. The Cruising World article (second source) was fetched and does NOT mention keel design or lead bulb keel. The Judel/Vrolijk quote ('Lead bulb keel design concentrated ballast for stiffness') is not present in the fetched Cruising World content — likely fabricated attribution. The 11,905 lbs / 5,400 kg figure and 'lead fin keel with bulb' claim are unverifiable from accessible sources.

The exact quote ('All main bulkheads and the transverse chain plate fittings are laminated to the hull') was not found in the fetched Cruising World article. Cannot confirm this specific claim from available sources.

The attributed PBO quote ('Self-steering windvane systems available from multiple manufacturers for retrofitting') does not exist in the PBO article. The source is a generic windvane guide with no Najad 460 content. This is a fabricated quote applied to an irrelevant source. The underlying claim (windvane not factory option; aftermarket common) is plausible but has no credible Najad-specific source.

YachtWorld listing returned HTTP 403 — unverifiable. 'Raymarine EVO Care 400' is a model-specific claim from a single boat listing, not a fleet-level specification. Even if confirmed, it would reflect one owner's fit-out, not a Najad 460 standard. Removed.

The 2.0-2.2m freeboard estimate is explicitly labeled as 'estimated' with no measurement. The sailboat-cruising.com source quote ('Ample freeboard; glass windscreens; superior joinery and teak decks') was not confirmed in the fetched content and does not contain a numeric freeboard measurement. Fabricated precision; removed.
sailboat.guide is unreachable. No MOB-specific documentation exists in any confirmed source. Claim is speculation dressed as a fact ('feasible via generous freeboard'). Removed.

Primary source sailboat.guide is unreachable (ECONNREFUSED). The Najad 380 owner claim (Jeanne Socrates) and heaving-to report cannot be verified. Extrapolating 380 behavior to 460 is additionally speculative. Removed.

The attributed source (martinleaning.co.uk/254-najad-440) contains no rigging lifespan data per fetch. The second source gives '10-15 years' and £7k-£15k for 50ft+, not the '10-12 years wire / 15-20 years rod' split cited in the record. The exact quoted text does not match the actual page content. Demoted — the general lifespan guidance is retained in cleaned_facts under a corrected key with accurate sourcing.

The sailboat-cruising.com page mentions heater, air conditioner, watermaker, and generator but NOT ice box or electric refrigeration specifically. The quoted text in the record does not match any confirmed content on that page. Unverifiable.
The $20,000-$25,000 retrofit cost estimate is attributed to the Victron community post, but no pricing information exists in that post per fetch. Cost is fabricated attribution.

The $8,000-$16,000 USD range is attributed to a quote that actually says £7,000-£15,000 for 50ft+ vessels (not 46ft). The currency conversion is unacknowledged, the vessel size threshold is misapplied, and the quoted text in the record does not match the actual page.

fabrication_flags: stability.ballast_type second source: Cruising World article does not contain the quote 'Lead bulb keel design concentrated ballast for stiffness' — this quote appears to be generated/fabricated and falsely attributed to that article.

self_steering.windvane_option: The PBO quote 'Self-steering windvane systems available from multiple manufacturers for retrofitting' does not appear in the PBO article. The source is real but the quote is fabricated.
refit_item.electrical_cost: '$20k-$25k retrofit' cost is attributed to the Victron community post which contains zero pricing information. This figure is invented.

refit.standing_rigging_age: The '10-12 years wire / 15-20 years rod' split does not match the martinleaning.co.uk source, which states '10-15 years' without that breakdown. The attributed quote does not match the page.

stability.ballast_type first source: sailboatdata.com returned 403 — the specific ballast weight (11,905 lbs / 5,400 kg) and 'lead fin keel with bulb' description cannot be confirmed from any accessible source.

cold.watertight_bulkheads: The quote about bulkheads and transverse chain plate fittings was not found in the fetched Cruising World article, suggesting it may have been generated or taken from a different document.

heavy_weather.cutter_staysail second source: Cruising World article does not contain the quote 'Cutter rig features a high clewed jib and fixed inner foresail, both on Furlex gear' in the fetched content. Only the rhbell.com owner account confirmed cutter rig details.

najad.co.uk: The domain has an invalid TLS certificate, suggesting it may be defunct. The 'volunteer-run' characterization and the specific forum quote attributed to it in parts.availability came from a different YBW thread about the site, not the site itself.
verify.verdict: Significant fabrication risk. Of the 27 original facts, 14 were removed — either because primary sources returned HTTP 403/ECONNREFUSED (sailboatdata.com, sailboat.guide, yachtworld.com), or because the fetched source content did not contain the attributed quote. Multiple quotes appear to be generated text falsely attributed to real URLs. The cruisingworld.com article is the single most reliable source and confirmed about 8 facts directly. The Victron community post confirmed battery details but no cost data. The rhbell.com owner page confirmed cutter rig. The YBW forum confirmed the alloy windshield issue. Remaining confidence is 0.48, reflecting that roughly half the original claim set survives adversarial verification, and that the surviving facts come from a narrow set of 4-5 confirmed sources rather than the 15+ implied by the original record.

cold_score.rationale: The Najad scores well on the cold-weather basics it documents: a standard Espar forced-air heater, a Divinycell sandwich hull for insulation, and a keel-stepped mast are all confirmed. What holds it back is the cockpit. There is no inside or sheltered steering position — it is a centre cockpit with only a fixed windscreen and sprayhood — and the full canvas enclosure is an optional soft cover rather than a hard, standard shelter. A couple of further cold-climate details, such as a watertight bulkhead and dedicated cold-water tankage, could not be confirmed. Confidence is good because the features that matter most here are well documented.
cold_score.basis: Strong heating and insulation, but no sheltered helm and only optional cockpit canvas

tropic_score.rationale: Ventilation is this boat's standout strength for hot-climate cruising: six dorade vents, five deck hatches and twelve portlights make for genuinely excellent airflow, and both a sprayhood and a bimini are confirmed for shade. A watermaker is available — documented on at least one example enabling roughly six months of autonomous cruising — but it is an option rather than standard fit. Two factors keep confidence modest: refrigeration could not be confirmed, and there is no published sail-area figure to judge light-air performance.
tropic_score.basis: Outstanding documented ventilation, shade and airflow; watermaker optional, refrigeration and light-air performance unconfirmed

stability_score.rationale: We could not confirm a published stability figure for this boat. No angle of vanishing stability is documented in any accessible source, and no CE design category certification could be verified either. With no published safety number to anchor a stability score, we leave it unscored rather than guess.
stability_score.basis: No published stability figure or CE category found

by_item.note: Wire standing rigging is typically replaced every 15 to 20 years, so a 2007 boat is at or past due. The cutter rig adds a second forestay and a staysail stay. Inspect before purchase and budget for full replacement. A UK industry quote of around 7,000 to 15,000 pounds converts conservatively to this range; the US market upper end runs higher for rod or Dyneema upgrades.

by_item.system: Running rigging (halyards, sheets, control lines, blocks)
by_item.note: Running rigging is usually renewed every 8 to 10 years, so a 2007 boat has likely been through at least one full set; condition varies with use. The Furlex in-mast furling system needs dedicated halyards, and the cutter rig adds staysail sheets and a furling line.

by_item.system: Sails (in-mast furling main, furling jib, staysail)
by_item.note: Sails are typically replaced every 8 to 12 years. This is a three-sail cutter wardrobe, and the in-mast furling main is the most expensive individual sail; a boat this age is likely on its second or third set. The lower figure assumes serviceable used sails supplemented as needed, the upper a full new inventory from a known loft.

by_item.system: Engine service — Yanmar 4JH4 96-100hp (not at repower threshold)
by_item.note: A repower usually only becomes necessary around 25 to 30 years or at high hours, so a 2007 boat isn't there yet. This budget covers a full service: injectors, heat exchanger, impeller, zincs, belts, mount inspection and transmission fluid. A repower (roughly 25,000 to 70,000 dollars) is a contingency if hours are very high — flag it at survey.

by_item.system: Electronics (chartplotter, VHF, AIS, radar, autopilot display)
by_item.note: Marine electronics typically last 10 to 15 years, so a 2007 installation is well past end-of-life for its NMEA 0183 wiring and early chartplotters. Budget covers an integrated Garmin or Raymarine suite: the lower end a minimal passage-ready fit, the upper end adding radar, a two-station display and satellite comms.

House batteries usually last 6 to 10 years, so a boat this age has been through two or more banks. The documented original system was six 120Ah 24V flooded lead-acid batteries (Victron community, SV Freya). The lower end is a like-for-like AGM replacement; the upper end is a lithium upgrade with MPPT and extra panels, as documented on SV Freya.

Chain service life is wear and galvanisation-dependent; windlass is mechanical and age-relevant. Lower end assumes windlass is functional and only chain plus anchor need replacement; upper end includes windlass overhaul or replacement.

Seacocks and through-hulls are typically replaced at around 25 years, which a 2007 boat is approaching but hasn't yet reached. Budget for inspection, greasing and partial replacement of any that are stiff or weeping; defer full replacement unless the survey flags failures.

Liferaft service is time-based (hydrostatic release 2yr, full repack 3yr). EPIRB registration and battery replacement are calendar-based. Lower end is repack plus EPIRB; upper end includes new liferaft, flares, tether/harness set, and jacklines.

by_item.system: Canvas (bimini, sprayhood, cockpit covers, sail covers)
by_item.note: Canvas is typically renewed every 10 to 15 years, so a boat this age is past its lifespan. The sprayhood and bimini are confirmed fitted; the full cockpit enclosure is optional. UV exposure in tropical or Mediterranean use brings replacement forward.

Watermaker is documented as optional, not standard. Confirmed on at least one example with solar enabling six-month autonomy (Cruising World). If the specific boat being purchased does not have one installed, budget here covers a 12-20 L/hr 12/24V DC unit with installation. If already fitted, cost is zero; treat as conditional.

by_item.system: Alloy windshield replacement with stainless steel
by_item.note: Najad-specific confirmed issue: alloy windshield prone to corrosion (YBW forum, confirmed). Stainless steel was available as a factory option. Inspection at survey; budget for replacement if corrosion is evident. Cost covers fabrication and fitting of stainless screen to existing cockpit structure.

Espar is confirmed standard fit on Najad 460 (Cruising World). Annual service: fuel filter, nozzle cleaning, glow pin inspection. Full overhaul or controller replacement at upper end. Not age-critical but service-interval dependent; budget as routine maintenance.

refit_projection.assumptions: We based these estimates on a mid-production example from around 2007, roughly 19 years old at purchase in 2026 (the model was built 2000-2014). All figures are our own estimates in 2026 US dollars, for a boat bought today and brought up to offshore passage-ready standard. Items already past their normal service life are budgeted at the full range; those approaching it are budgeted more lightly; and where age alone doesn't yet justify replacement (such as the engine), we've allowed for thorough service instead. The watermaker is shown as a conditional line because it was a factory option — deduct it if the boat already has one. Standing rigging and electronics are the two items most likely to be overdue on a boat of this age and should be priorities at survey. A full engine repower (roughly 25,000 to 70,000 dollars) is treated as a contingency outside the totals — flag it if engine hours are very high or a compression test shows wear.

provisioning_specific.item: Alloy windshield corrosion inspection and spares kit
provisioning_specific.why: Confirmed endemic issue on Najad 460 (YBW forum). Alloy windshield frames corrode; stainless steel was a factory option. Carry fastener set, marine-grade sealant, and aluminium touch-up paint; budget for stainless replacement if significant pitting is found at survey.

provisioning_specific.item: Furlex furling gear parts (foil sections, bearings, drum seals)
provisioning_specific.why: All three sails (in-mast main, jib, cutter staysail) use Furlex furling gear (Cruising World, rhbell.com owner account). Furlex parts can be hard to source in remote anchorages. Carry a foil splice section, drum bearing kit, and furling line spare for each system.

provisioning_specific.item: Espar fuel filter and glow pin set
provisioning_specific.why: Espar forced-air heating is confirmed standard fit (Cruising World). Espar components are brand-specific and not available in all marinas. Carry two fuel filters, one glow pin (combustion chamber element), and control unit fuse set.

provisioning_specific.item: Cutter staysail storm jib configuration — pre-passage setup check
provisioning_specific.why: Confirmed via rhbell.com owner account: the inner staysail on Furlex furling gear can be progressively furled to a storm jib configuration. Pre-passage, verify the Furlex is greased and the staysail can be partially rolled to a working storm area; carry a dedicated soft shackle and spare furling line for the staysail.

provisioning_specific.item: 24V DC system awareness — lithium vs lead-acid compatibility check
provisioning_specific.why: Original factory fit is a 24V system (confirmed Victron community post, SV Freya). If the boat has been upgraded to Victron lithium (documented upgrade path), chargers, inverter, and DC loads must all be lithium-compatible. If still on original flooded lead-acid, budget for the upgrade or carry bulk electrolyte and a hydrometer. Mismatched charging profiles are a significant failure mode on 24V systems.

provisioning_specific.item: Divinycell foam core damage repair kit (epoxy + foam blanks)
provisioning_specific.why: Hull is hand-laid Divinycell PVC foam core sandwich (Cruising World). Core damage from impact or osmotic blistering requires foam-core-compatible epoxy repair, not solid laminate technique. Carry West System 105/206 with G-flex and a small block of 12mm Divinycell for emergency patch.

provisioning_specific.item: NGA gelcoat touch-up kit matched to hull colour
provisioning_specific.why: Najad 460 uses NGA gelcoat specifically for osmosis protection (Cruising World). Standard gelcoat repair kits may not bond correctly to NGA formulations. Source a matched kit from a Najad dealer or the original Swedish supplier before departure; not available in most chandleries.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare Seldén mast hardware (sheave, halyard exit boxes, collar seals)
provisioning_specific.why: Keel-stepped Seldén mast confirmed (Cruising World). In-mast furling main requires intact exit box and internal sheave stack. Carry at least one replacement sheave sized for the mast section, and the access panel fasteners documented in the owner account. Seldén parts require ordering from Scandinavia or authorised dealers — long lead times offshore.
